changeReason: Improved translation from Japanese. Old: "Research Notes Ⅱ"

TextENGNew: <On Oyashiro-sama>
changeReason: Old translation omits 'について' (about/on), rendering it as just a name label rather than a topic heading; new correctly indicates the section is 'about' Oyashiro-sama.

TextENGNew: As for Oyashiro-sama, few people actually know how the name is written.

This is because across different eras, various honorifics have been appended and the name has subtly shifted, with different characters used to write it,@kmaking it extremely difficult to determine the original, correct name.
changeReason: Old uses 'he' to gender Oyashiro-sama (not in JPN) and simplifies to 'nobody knows the real one'; new avoids gendering and more accurately conveys the difficulty of determining the original name.

TextENGNew: The only constant across all periods is that the four syllables "O-ya-shi-ro" always appear in the reading.

There is a theory that the shrine -- or@byashiro.@< "社" @>-- dedicated to Oyashiro-sama itself became an object of worship,@kand that people came to call it@bo-yashiro.@< "御社," @>or "the honorable shrine" -- but this lacks any real insight.
changeReason: Old renders 'センスの欠片もない' as 'doesn't make a bit of sense' (implying logical incoherence), while new uses 'lacks any real insight' (implying intellectual shallowness), which better matches the JPN critique of the theory's lack of imagination.

TextENGNew: I do not know whether this is related, but it is said that the Furude family -- the most exalted bloodline tasked with worshiping Oyashiro-sama -- actually carries his blood.
And according to a legend passed down through the Furude family, if for eight consecutive generations the firstborn child is a girl, that eighth daughter will be the reincarnation of Oyashiro-sama.

If we follow this legend, the correct characters for Oyashiro-sama's name would be "御八代さま" -- "御" for "honorable," "八" for "eight," and "代" for "generation."@k(These character assignments are purely my own conjecture; there is no way to verify them.)
If this is so, then the name Oyashiro-sama was coined on the premise that he would one day return.
The second coming of a deity is found in several religions and is not particularly unusual.
However, it should not be forgotten that in some of those religions, the deity's return is called the "Day of Judgment" and signifies the end of the world.
Rika Furude -- a girl so adored by the village elders that they would do anything for her.
...Rumors say she is that eighth generation -- Oyashiro-sama incarnate.
I do not know the Furude family tree, but I can confirm that for at least the past two generations, the firstborn child has been a girl.
Rika Furude, who watches over Hinamizawa.
If the village were to lose her protection, what would happen?
Would the harmony between humans and demons be shattered?
Would there once again arise a hell where man-eating demons run rampant?
A gruesome feast -- demons who have remembered how delicious those frail humans were, humans who can only weep, scream, and flee in terror.
...What would such a sight look like?
Just imagining it... makes my heart race.
